mission.title: Install and Configure Redis Operator (Spotahome / OT-CONTAINER-KIT)
mission.description: The Redis Operator by OT-CONTAINER-KIT simplifies the deployment and management of Redis on Kubernetes, supporting standalone, cluster, and sentinel modes. It is ideal for organizations looking to leverage Redis in a Kubernetes environment with built-in monitoring and best practices.

steps.title: Install Redis Operator
steps.description:
Use Helm to install the Redis Operator. Ensure you have Helm v3.x installed. Run the following commands:

```bash
# Add the Helm repository
helm repo add opstree https://opstree.github.io/redis-operator

# Update the Helm repository
helm repo update

# Install the Redis Operator
helm install redis-operator opstree/redis-operator --version v0.19.0
```

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.description:
Check if the Redis Operator is running correctly:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n default -l app=redis-operator
```

steps.title: Create a Redis Cluster
steps.description:
Create a Redis cluster by applying a custom resource definition (CRD). Save the following YAML to `redis-cluster.yaml`:

```yaml
apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
    password: "yourpassword"
```

Then apply the configuration:

```bash
kubectl apply -f redis-cluster.yaml
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring for your Redis cluster using the built-in redis-exporter. Ensure you have the necessary RBAC permissions set up:

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OT-CONTAINER-KIT/redis-operator/main/deploy/rbac.yaml
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall Redis Operator
uninstall.description:
To uninstall the Redis Operator and clean up resources, run:

```bash
helm uninstall redis-operator
kubectl delete crd redis.opstree.dev
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Redis Operator
upgrade.description:
To upgrade from v0.18 to v0.19, first backup your Redis data. Then run:

```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade redis-operator opstree/redis-operator --version v0.19.0
```

Verify the upgrade:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n default -l app=redis-operator
```

troubleshooting.title: Redis Operator Pod CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Redis Operator pod is in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs:

```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n default
```

Common issues include misconfigured CRDs or insufficient permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Redis Cluster Not Starting
troubleshooting.description:
If your Redis cluster is not starting, check the status of the Redis CR:

```bash
kubectl describe redis my-redis-cluster
```

Ensure that the Redis image version is compatible and the password is correctly set.

troubleshooting.title: Monitoring Not Working
troubleshooting.description:
If monitoring is not functioning, verify that the redis-exporter is running:

```bash
kubectl get pods -n default -l app=redis-exporter
```

Check the RBAC permissions if the exporter is not starting.

versionNotes.changes: Improved monitoring capabilities and support for Redis 7.x.
versionNotes.deprecations: Support for Redis 5.x has been deprecated.

versionNotes.changes: Initial support for Redis cluster mode and enhanced RBAC configurations.

resolution.summary: A successful setup includes a running Redis Operator pod, a deployed Redis cluster, and operational monitoring. You can verify the installation by checking the status of the pods and ensuring that the Redis cluster is accessible.
codeSnippets:
# Add the Helm repository
helm repo add opstree https://opstree.github.io/redis-operator

# Update the Helm repository
helm repo update

# Install the Redis Operator
helm install redis-operator opstree/redis-operator --version v0.19.0

apiVersion: redis.opstree.dev/v1
kind: Redis
metadata:
  name: my-redis-cluster
spec:
  mode: cluster
  replicas: 3
  redis:
    image: redis:7.0.0
    password: "yourpassword"

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl configured to access it.
